title: Lexico-Grammar Polish — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Nominalization + hedging + dependent prepositions + advanced punctuation + comparatives — the final 10%
intro: Once you control tenses, conditionals, articles and clauses, the final 10% of Band 7+ comes from the polish: turning verbs into noun phrases (nominalization), softening absolute claims (hedging), pairing the right preposition with each verb/adjective, deploying semicolons and dashes correctly, and using comparatives with sophistication. Each one is small. Together, they're the difference between Band 7 and Band 8.

sections.title: 1. Nominalization — Verbs Into Noun Phrases
sections.summary: Academic writing prefers noun phrases over clauses. Nominalization compresses information and creates the dense, formal register Band 7+ examiners reward.

examples.plural: the development of
an increase in / a decrease in
a rise in / a fall in
the introduction of
the implementation of
the decision to
the failure to / the failure of
the success of
the discovery of
the analysis of
improvement / the improvement of
the contribution of
the dependence of … on
the investigation of / into
the approval of
The economy grew rapidly. →
The rapid growth of the economy was striking.
The government decided to invest in schools. →
The government's decision to invest in schools was widely supported.
Pollution has increased. This is concerning. →
The increase in pollution is a matter of growing concern.

items: an increase IN (NOT 'of'): an increase in pollution
a decrease IN: a decrease in birth rates
a rise IN: a rise in unemployment
a fall IN: a fall in living standards
the introduction OF: the introduction of new policies
the implementation OF: the implementation of reforms
the development OF: the development of technology
the impact OF: the impact of climate change ON society
the effect OF: the effect of advertising ON children
the cause OF: the cause of the crisis
the reason FOR: the reason for the delay
the importance OF: the importance of education
the role OF: the role of women in society
rules.note: Wrong preposition is a subtle but visible error: ❌ 'an increase OF pollution' should be 'an increase IN pollution'.

examples.plural: Cities are growing very quickly, which means more people live in them. This means there is more pressure on housing.
The rapid growth of urban populations has placed considerable pressure on housing markets.

rules.note: Same idea — half the words, twice the density. This is exactly what 'wide range with full flexibility' means in the Band 8 descriptor.

sections.tip: When you write a sentence with a -ing verb (Pollution is increasing in cities), try: 'The increase in pollution in cities is...'. If it reads naturally, use the nominalised form. Don't over-do it — alternate verb and noun forms for rhythm.

sections.title: 2. Hedging Language — Academic Caution
sections.summary: Academic writing avoids absolute claims. Hedging softens certainty so claims sound measured and credible. Band 7+ writers hedge naturally; Band 6 writers make sweeping claims.

examples.plural: Such policies MAY have unintended consequences.
This trend COULD pose a serious threat.
Excessive screen time CAN be harmful.

items: tend to + V (Children tend to learn quickly)
appear to + V (The data appears to support this view)
seem to + V (Younger generations seem to prefer digital media)
be likely to + V (Air pollution is likely to worsen)
in most cases / in many cases
to some extent / to a certain degree / to a large extent
the possibility of / the likelihood of

rules.pattern: Hedging in Task 2 — before vs after

examples.plural: All teenagers are addicted to phones. Phones destroy social skills.
Many teenagers appear to be heavily reliant on their phones, which may be undermining face-to-face social skills.
It might possibly tend to perhaps suggest that some people may possibly...
It tends to suggest that some people may...

rules.note: One or two hedges per claim is enough. Stacking 4+ hedges sounds vague and unconfident. Hedge in EVIDENCE; be firm in your OPINION.

sections.tip: When you express your OPINION in Task 2, be FIRM. When you describe EVIDENCE or CAUSE, hedge. 'I firmly believe X' (firm opinion) + 'because Y may contribute to Z' (hedged evidence). This balance is exactly Band 7+.

sections.title: 3. Dependent Prepositions — Verb / Adjective / Noun + Preposition
sections.summary: Many words require a specific preposition. Wrong preposition is the most common lexical-grammar error in Band 6 writing.

items: similar TO / different FROM (NOT 'than' in formal BrE)
agree WITH (a person) / agree ON (a topic) / agree TO (a proposal)
blame sb FOR sth / blame sth ON sb
argue WITH (a person) / ABOUT (a topic) / FOR (in favour) / AGAINST
look FOR / AFTER / INTO / AT / UP

examples.plural: ✔ It depends ON the situation.
✔ Different FROM (formal / BrE / IELTS norm).
✔ The reason FOR the problem.
✔ A solution TO the issue.
✔ An effect ON health.
✔ An increase IN pollution.
✔ Discuss the problem. ('discuss' takes no preposition!)
✔ Mention the issue. ('mention' takes no preposition!)
✔ I'm married TO her.
(actually correct) ✔ They escaped FROM prison. — but ❌ 'They escaped OF the prison' is wrong.

rules.pattern: Verbs that take NO preposition (Band 6 over-prepositions)
items: approach (NOT 'approach to' as a verb — 'approach the problem')
lack (NOT 'lack of' as verb — 'they lack experience')
demand (NOT 'demand for' as verb — 'they demand action')

sections.tip: Build a personal cheat-sheet of the 30 dependent prepositions you misuse most. Drill 5/day. After 14 days, the right preposition becomes automatic — and you eliminate the most-noticed lexical-grammar errors at the Band 7 threshold.

sections.title: 4. Advanced Punctuation — Semicolons, Colons, Dashes
sections.summary: Beyond commas and full stops. Used correctly, these signal Band 7+ control of sentence rhythm.

rules.pattern: SEMICOLON (;) — joining two related independent clauses

examples.plural: Many students struggle with grammar; few are taught it explicitly.
Demand has surged; supply chains are strained.

rules.note: Both sides of a semicolon must be FULL sentences. ❌ 'Many students struggle; especially with grammar.' (right side isn't a complete sentence).

rules.pattern: SEMICOLON in complex lists (where items contain commas)

examples.plural: The committee met in three cities: London, England; Paris, France; and Berlin, Germany.

rules.pattern: COLON (:) — introducing a list, explanation, or quotation

examples.plural: There are three causes: poor diet, lack of exercise, and stress.
The result was clear: the policy had failed.
Einstein said it best: 'Imagination is more important than knowledge.'

rules.note: ❌ Don't use a colon after a verb or preposition: 'The causes are: poor diet…' should be 'The causes are poor diet, lack of exercise, and stress.'

rules.pattern: EM DASH (—) — embedded info OR strong afterthought

examples.plural: Most countries — including developed nations — face this problem.
The plan is bold — perhaps too bold.

rules.note: Use sparingly: max one paired use and one single use per essay. Em dash is for emphasis, not a casual replacement for commas.

rules.pattern: COMMA — top Band 7+ comma rules to master
items: After introductory phrases: 'In recent years, pollution has worsened.'
Around non-defining relative clauses: 'My brother, who lives in Canada, is a doctor.'
Between coordinated clauses (FANBOYS): 'I studied hard, but I failed.'
In a list of three or more (Oxford comma optional but Band 7+ writers often use it): 'apples, oranges, and bananas'
After fronted concession: 'Despite the difficulties, we continued.'
NOT before subordinator at sentence end: 'I left because I was tired.' (no comma)

examples.singular: ❌ Comma splice (joining two clauses with only comma)
examples.plural: ❌ Pollution is increasing, governments must act. → ✔ Pollution is increasing; governments must act. / Pollution is increasing. Governments must act. / Pollution is increasing, AND governments must act.
❌ The reasons are: economic, social and political. → ✔ The reasons are economic, social and political. / There are three reasons: economic, social and political.
❌ In recent years pollution has worsened. → ✔ In recent years, pollution has worsened.

sections.tip: Add ONE semicolon and ONE colon to every Task 2 essay deliberately. Don't force them — find natural places. After two weeks of conscious use, they become automatic.

sections.title: 5. Comparatives & Superlatives — Beyond 'Bigger' and 'Best'
sections.summary: Basic comparatives are Band 5. Band 7+ uses sophisticated comparison structures.

items: 2-syllable ending in -y: y → ier / iest (happier, happiest)
2+ syllables: more / most (more interesting, most beautiful)
Irregular: good → better → best, bad → worse → worst, far → farther/further → farthest/furthest

examples.plural: slightly / a little / a bit / marginally + comparative — slightly more expensive, marginally better
rather / somewhat / fairly + comparative — rather more challenging
much / far / a lot + comparative — much more important, far better, a lot more efficient
considerably / significantly + comparative — considerably faster, significantly more accurate
Education is BY FAR THE most important factor.
This is THE VERY BEST option available.
Climate change is EASILY THE biggest challenge of our time.
The more you practise, the better you become.
The longer the journey, the more tired we get.
The less we sleep, the worse we perform.

rules.note: This structure is high-yield Band 7+ — drop one in any Task 2 essay where two trends are linked.

examples.plural: Online learning can be as effective as classroom teaching.
The new policy is twice as effective as the old one.
Demand has risen three times as fast as supply.
Cities are getting bigger and bigger.
Technology is becoming increasingly sophisticated.

examples.singular: ❌ More taller / more better / more worse
examples.plural: ✔ Taller / better / worse (irregulars; never 'more' before -er forms)

examples.singular: ❌ The most tallest / the most best
examples.plural: ✔ The tallest / the best (don't combine 'most' with -est)
✔ Different FROM (formal BrE / IELTS norm)

examples.singular: ❌ Many people prefer rural life THAN city life.
examples.plural: ✔ Many people prefer rural life TO city life. (prefer X to Y)

sections.title: 6. Specialised Lexico-Grammar for Task 1 (Data)
sections.summary: Task 1 has its own vocabulary system. Master these collocations.

items: Upward: rise / increase / grow / climb / surge / soar / rocket / shoot up
Downward: fall / decrease / decline / drop / plunge / plummet / dip
Stable: remain stable / level off / stabilise / plateau / stay constant
Fluctuation: fluctuate / vary / oscillate / show fluctuations
Sharp: sharply / steeply / dramatically / significantly / considerably

examples.plural: Sales rose sharply / increased dramatically / surged unexpectedly.
Numbers gradually declined / steadily decreased.
Prices remained stable / levelled off / plateaued at $50.
Sales rose sharply.
There was a sharp rise in sales.
Production declined slightly.
A slight decline was observed in production.

rules.note: Mixing verb and noun forms creates variety. Three verb-form sentences in a row = Band 6. Mix in noun forms for Band 7+.

sections.title: 7. Spelling Patterns That Cost Marks
sections.summary: Spelling errors fall under Lexical Resource. The patterns below are the most common Band 7→6.5 sources.

examples.plural: stop → stopped / stopping; plan → planned / planning; run → running
begin → beginning; prefer → preferred; admit → admitting
open → opened / opening; happen → happened; benefit → benefited
study → studied / studies; happy → happier / happiest
play → played; key → keys (no change)

rules.pattern: Silent E dropped before vowel suffix

examples.plural: make → making; come → coming; have → having

examples.singular: keep the e before consonant suffix
examples.plural: useful, lovely, careful

items: ❌ recieve → ✔ receive (i before e except after c)

rules.pattern: British vs American spelling — pick one and be consistent

examples.plural: behaviour / behavior, colour / color, favour / favor
centre / center, theatre / theater
realise / realize, organise / organize, recognise / recognize
travelled / traveled, cancelled / canceled

rules.note: IELTS accepts BOTH variants — but be CONSISTENT throughout one essay. Mixing 'colour' and 'color' in the same essay is an accuracy slip.

sections.title: 8. Top Lexico-Grammar Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: The cumulative polish — 10 specific errors that pull Band 7 candidates down.

examples.singular: ❌ depends OF / different THAN / reason OF / solution OF
examples.plural: ✔ depends ON / different FROM / reason FOR / solution TO

examples.singular: ❌ We will discuss about the issue.
examples.plural: ✔ We will discuss the issue. (no preposition)
✔ an increase IN pollution
✔ the impact ON society

examples.singular: ❌ It might possibly tend to perhaps suggest that some people may possibly...
examples.plural: ✔ It tends to suggest that some people may...

rules.pattern: 5. Comma splice (two clauses joined by comma only)

examples.singular: ❌ Pollution is rising, governments must act.
examples.plural: ✔ Pollution is rising. Governments must act. / Pollution is rising; governments must act. / Pollution is rising, AND governments must act.

examples.singular: ❌ The reasons are: economic and social.
examples.plural: ✔ The reasons are economic and social. / There are two reasons: economic and social.
✔ taller / better
✔ the easiest / simplest

rules.pattern: 8. Mixed BrE / AmE spelling within one essay

examples.singular: ❌ The colour reflects the favorite tradition...
examples.plural: ✔ Pick one variety: 'colour... favourite...' (BrE) OR 'color... favorite...' (AmE).

rules.pattern: 9. Common spelling errors on IELTS topics

examples.plural: ✔ environment / government / opportunity / accommodation

rules.pattern: 10. 'Prefer X than Y' instead of 'prefer X to Y'

examples.plural: ✔ I prefer tea TO coffee. / I would rather have tea THAN coffee. (different structure)

sections.tip: Build a personal 'top 30 errors' card — your specific recurring slips. Audit every essay against this card. After two weeks of conscious checking, most of these errors disappear.

checklist: I use nominalization at least 3 times per Task 2 essay (the rise in, the development of, the impact of).
I use the correct preposition with each nominalization (increase IN, impact ON, solution TO, reason FOR).
I hedge claims with modal verbs, lexical hedges (tend to, appear to), or adverbs (often, in many cases).
I never over-hedge — one or two hedges per claim, never four.
I am firm in my opinion ('I firmly believe') and hedged in my evidence ('Y may contribute to').
I never write 'depends OF / different THAN / discuss about / mention about / married with'.
I use semicolons to join related independent clauses (one or two per essay).
I use colons to introduce lists / explanations after a complete clause (not after a verb).
I never write a comma splice (two clauses joined by only a comma).
I use modifiers with comparatives (significantly more, considerably less, by far the most).
I deploy 'the more X, the more Y' parallel comparison at least once per Task 2 essay.
I am consistent with BrE or AmE spelling throughout one essay.
I have eliminated my top 10 personal spelling errors through targeted drilling.
